programElements.mission: This program provides the foundational engineering, analytic rigor, and IT development required for U.S. Cyber Command (USCYBERCOM) to plan, direct, and synchronize full‑spectrum cyberspace operations. These investments enable the nation to defend critical infrastructure, impose costs on adversaries in cyberspace, and ensure the Joint Force can operate with confidence in a contested digital environment.

The U.S. Cyber Command (USCYBERCOM) conducts full‑spectrum cyberspace operations to deter or defeat strategic threats to United States interests and critical infrastructure, ensure mission assurance for the operation and defense of the Department of War Information Environment (DoWIE), and support the achievement of Joint Force objectives. The Robust Infrastructure Program Element provides the platforms, operational environments, and enabling infrastructure required to conduct both Offensive Cyberspace Operations (OCO) and Defensive Cyberspace Operations (DCO). These capabilities ensure the Cyber Mission Force (CMF) can generate, deploy, and sustain cyberspace effects in support of national and combatant commander requirements. The Joint Common Access Platform (JCAP) is USCYBERCOM’s enterprise platform for executing Title 10 OCO. JCAP provides the CMF with a standardized, resilient, and scalable operational environment that enables mission agility, minimizes detection, and ensures precise attribution against approved targets. With the transition of all legacy Service‑specific platforms complete, JCAP is now focused on modernization to deliver the next‑generation platform, incorporating enhanced resilience, operational scalability, and advanced artificial intelligence‑enabled capabilities. The program element also supports DCO through managed unclassified infrastructure that provides virtual tenant environments for Cyber National Mission Force (CNMF) Hunt Forward Operations (HFO). These environments enable CNMF teams to conduct remote defensive missions, analyze partner‑provided data, and identify malicious cyber activity. Robust Infrastructure includes resources for the Cyberspace Rapid Response Platform (CRRP), which provides the CNMF with the ability to rapidly implement new capabilities, tradecraft, and services in response to emergent or crisis scenarios. CRRP enables Joint Task Force ZERO to accelerate access to adversary networks, generate actionable intelligence, and disseminate insights across the Joint Force to counter adversary aggression. In the cyber domain, readiness is perishable. The skills, tools, and teamwork required to counter advanced adversaries demand continuous, high‑fidelity training. The Persistent Cyber Training Environment (PCTE) is the Department of War’s enterprise solution to this challenge, providing a standardized, on‑demand training platform essential for building and certifying the readiness of the Cyber Mission Force (CMF) at the individual, team, and force levels. PCTE delivers a common training capability that maximizes shared content across the Military Services. The platform emulates realistic network environments, integrates with other cyber ranges and training systems, and enables complex, scenario‑based training events that replicate operational conditions. PCTE is managed by the Capability Program Executive for Simulation, Training, Test and Threats (CPE ST3), designated as the Department of War’s acquisition lead for the program. The program is directed by Section 1645 of the FY 2016 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA). PCTE achieved Milestone B on 06 December 2019 and subsequently received approval to transition from a Major Capability Acquisition program to the Software Acquisition Pathway Execution Phase, as documented in the Acquisition Decision Memorandum signed by the Army Acquisition Executive on 02 May 2025. An updated Cyber Capability Integration and Development System (CCIDS) validated Information System – Capability Development Document (IS‑CDD‑U) was approved on 08 January 2024. Through ongoing rapid prototyping and iterative development, PCTE has delivered a standardized CMF training capability since the release of PCTE Version 2 in FY 2020 and continues to evolve through a continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) approach. PE 0208086JCY: Cyber Training Environment UNCLASSIFIED Volume 5 - 9 United States Cyber Command Page 1 of 11 R-1 Line #137

Dominance in cyberspace requires commanders to visualize the battlespace, make informed decisions faster than the adversary, and direct forces with precision. Joint Cyber Command and Control (JCC2) provides the integrated situational awareness, planning, and battle management capabilities required to achieve these outcomes. As a congressionally directed effort, JCC2 serves as the Department’s focal point for unifying cyber Command and Control (C2) across all echelons, enabling seamless coordination between Cyber Mission Forces (CMF), Combatant Commands, and joint, coalition, and interagency partners. The modern cyber environment is characterized by speed, complexity, and the convergence of operations across domains. Legacy and disconnected C2 systems cannot meet today’s operational tempo. JCC2 addresses this gap by delivering a single, integrated cyber C2 framework that ensures all stakeholders operate from a shared, authoritative operational picture. By synchronizing cyber C2 with joint and coalition C2 systems, JCC2 enhances multi‑domain operations, reduces planning timelines, improves decision quality, and shortens the kill chain. These capabilities directly support the evolving mission requirements of CMF teams conducting cyberspace operations. To maintain pace with rapidly evolving threats and operational demands, JCC2 employs a Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) development model. This approach accelerates prototyping, software development, integration of lab‑developed capabilities, and rigorous testing and evaluation. JCC2 activities include program management, studies and analysis, pilots and demonstrations, risk‑reduction efforts for emerging technologies, and the development and assessment of operational systems aligned to validated capability requirements. JCC2 is funded under Budget Activity 7, Operational System Development, reflecting its focus on upgrading, enhancing, and integrating systems that have been fielded or approved for full‑rate production. This budget request supports critical development efforts to ensure warfighters have the most advanced, interoperable, and effective cyber C2 capabilities available. Continued investment in JCC2 is essential to maintaining the nation’s competitive advantage in cyberspace, enabling the CMF to deter, disrupt, and defeat cyber threats in defense of U.S. national interests. PE 0208097JCY: Cyber Command and Control (C2) UNCLASSIFIED Volume 5 - 35 United States Cyber Command Page 1 of 10 R-1 Line #240

The Joint Cyber Warfighting Architecture (JCWA) cannot function without a foundational, cloud-based platform to host its capabilities and a secure method to move data between its various components. This program element delivers both: The Unified Platform (UP) provides the core infrastructure—the digital factory and fighting platform —for all of JCWA, while the Cross Domain Solution (CDS) provides the accredited, high-speed 'data highways' required to move critical intelligence and operational data securely across classification boundaries. UP provides the cloud-based infrastructure for the Joint Cyber Warfighting Architecture (JCWA) that enables integration of cyberspace operations capabilities, systems, data and analytics for Cyber Mission Forces. Cross Domain Solution (CDS) will deliver development and subject matter expertise to enable a scalable cross domain solution for JCWA based on industry best practices and requirements specified within National Cross Domain Strategy and Management Office (NCDSMO) Raise The Bar Strategy’s Baseline, and National Security Memorandum (NSM)-8 “Improving the Cybersecurity of National Security, Department of War and Intelligence Community Systems."

The U.S. Cyber Command (USCYBERCOM) conducts full spectrum cyberspace operations to deter or defeat strategic threats to U.S. interests, protect critical infrastructure, and ensure mission assurance for the Department of War (DoW) information environment. These operations enable Joint Force Commanders to maintain freedom of action in cyberspace while denying adversaries the ability to exploit or disrupt U.S. networks. USCYBERCOM’s cyber operations objectives include: • Deterring adversaries by undermining confidence in the reliability and security of their systems and capabilities. • Enhancing joint operations through intelligence collection before conflict, timely tipping and cueing during conflict, and post operation assessments. • Projecting power in cyberspace to access, influence, or disrupt adversary systems, providing non kinetic options to Combatant Commanders. • Disrupting, degrading, or destroying the capabilities of malicious cyber actors that threaten U.S. national security. To achieve these objectives, USCYBERCOM—working with the Military Services and other cyber stakeholders—develops, fields, and sustains the infrastructure, architectures, tools, and enabling technologies required to support the Cyber Mission Force (CMF). These investments ensure the CMF can conduct persistent engagement, defend the DoW Information Environment, and deliver operational effects across the full spectrum of cyberspace operations. The Cyber Operations Technology Support program provides the technical foundation, mission systems, and integration activities required to maintain CMF readiness and operational effectiveness. Funding supports the development, enhancement, and sustainment of capabilities aligned with the Joint Cyber Warfighting Architecture (JCWA), ensuring the CMF can counter rapidly evolving cyber threats and deliver timely, coordinated effects in support of Joint Force objectives. Continued investment is essential to sustaining U.S. cyber superiority and enabling the Joint Force to deter, disrupt, and defeat adversary cyber operations.
